title: Molecular monitoring of biofouling communities using settlement panels and diagnostic assays
author: Jack Longsden

steps.title: Add 200 μL of the supernatant...
steps.instruction: Add 200 μL of the supernatant from STEP 10 into each well containing Protein Denaturation Buffer and ethanol.

steps.title: Per 96-well plate, the Protein Denaturation...
steps.instruction: Per 96-well plate, the Protein Denaturation Buffer and ethanol master mix should comprise: A B C 1 Reagent Amount per sample Amount per 96-well plate 2 Protein Denaturation Buffer 220 μL 42.2 mL 3 Ethanol (100 %) 220 μL 42.2 μL

steps.title: Add all of the sample solution...
steps.instruction: Add all of the sample solution (~ 600 μL) to a well in a 96-well DNA membrane spin column and cover with a breathable seal. Ensure there is a suitable reservoir beneath into which the flow-through will go (e.g., 2.2 mL deep-well plate).

steps.title: Centrifuge at ≥ 6000 x g...
steps.instruction: Centrifuge at ≥ 6000 x g for 1 minute and discard the flow-through. If the centrifuge cannot reach 6000 x g , a longer centrifugation (e.g., 10 minutes) will work.

steps.title: Add 500 μL Wash Buffer 1...
steps.instruction: Add 500 μL Wash Buffer 1 to each spin column and cover with a breathable seal.

steps.title: Centrifuge at ≥ 6000 x g...
steps.instruction: Centrifuge at ≥ 6000 x g for 1 minute and discard the flow-through. If the centrifuge cannot reach 6000 x g , a longer centrifugation (e.g., 5 minutes) will work.

steps.title: Wash Buffer 1 should be comprised of the following reagents
steps.instruction: Wash Buffer 1 should be comprised of the following reagents: A B C D 1 Reagent Required concentration in buffer Chemical molarity Amount per 100 mL 2 Guanidine HCl 7 M 95.53 29.4 g 3 Ethanol 56 % - 56 mL 4 H2O - - 44 mL

steps.title: Add 500 μL Wash Buffer 2...
steps.instruction: Add 500 μL Wash Buffer 2 to each spin column and cover with a breathable seal.

steps.title: Wash Buffer 2 should be pH...
steps.instruction: Wash Buffer 2 should be pH ~7 and comprised of the following reagents: A B C D 1 Reagent Required concentration in buffer Chemical molarity Amount per 100 mL 2 Ethanol (100 %) 70 % 58.44 70 mL 3 1 M Tris-Hcl 10 mM - 1 mL 4 H2O - - 29 mL

steps.title: Centrifuge at 20,000 x g for...
steps.instruction: Centrifuge at 20,000 x g for 3 minutes and discard the flow-through. If the centrifuge cannot reach 20,000 x g , a longer centrifugation (e.g., 15 minutes) will work.

steps.title: Carefully move the spin column plate to a new 0
steps.instruction: Carefully move the spin column plate to a new 0.5 mL DNA collection plate. The liquid level following the final wash will be close to the base of the spin column, so take care not to let it touch to prevent ethanol transfer to the soon-to-be eluted DNA.

steps.title: Add 200 μL Elution Buffer directly...
steps.instruction: Add 200 μL Elution Buffer directly to the silica membrane and leave it at room temperature for 5 minutes, covering with a breathable seal.

steps.title: Elution Buffer should be pH ~7...
steps.instruction: Elution Buffer should be pH ~7 and comprised of the following reagents: A B C D 1 Reagent Required concentration in buffer Chemical molarity Amount per 100 mL 2 1 M Tris-Hcl 10 mM - 1 mL 3 H2O - - 99 mL

steps.title: Centrifuge at ≥ 6000 x g for 1 minute
steps.instruction: Centrifuge at ≥ 6000 x g for 1 minute. The DNA is now in the collection plate and can be taken forward to amplification. Steps 20-21 can be repeated for increased DNA yield but a lower overall concentration. If the centrifuge cannot reach 6000 x g , a longer centrifugation (e.g., 5 minutes) will work, although should not be necessary for this step.

steps.title: Add 400 μL of master mix...
steps.instruction: Add 400 μL of master mix of Protein Denaturation Buffer and ethanol (detailed below) to each well of the 2.2 ml 96-well plate.

steps.title: Protein Denaturation Buffer should be comprised...
steps.instruction: Protein Denaturation Buffer should be comprised of the following reagents: A B C D 1 Reagent Required concentration in buffer Chemical molarity Amount per 100 mL 2 Guanidine HCl 5 M 95.53 47.7 g 3 H2O - - 100 mL

steps.title: Per 96-well plate, the Protein Denaturation...
steps.instruction: Per 96-well plate, the Protein Denaturation Buffer and ethanol master mix should comprise: A B C 1 Reagent Amount per sample Amount per 96-well plate 2 Protein Denaturation Buffer 220 μL 21.1 mL 3 Ethanol (100 %) 220 μL 21.1 μL

steps.title: Add all of the sample solution...
steps.instruction: Add all of the sample solution (~ 600 μL) to a well in a 96-well silica membrane spin column and cover with a breathable seal. Ensure there is a suitable reservoir beneath into which the flow-through will go (e.g., 2.2 mL deep-well plate).

steps.title: Centrifuge at ≥ 6000 x g...
steps.instruction: Centrifuge at ≥ 6000 x g for 10 minutes and discard the flow-through. If the centrifuge cannot reach 6000 x g , a longer centrifugation (e.g., 10 minutes) will work.

steps.title: Add 500 μL Wash Buffer 1...
steps.instruction: Add 500 μL Wash Buffer 1 to each spin column and cover with a breathable seal.

steps.title: Wash Buffer 1 should be comprised of the following reagents
steps.instruction: Wash Buffer 1 should be comprised of the following reagents: A B C D 1 Reagent Required concentration in buffer Chemical molarity Amount per 100 mL 2 Guanidine HCl 7 M 95.53 29.4 g 3 Ethanol 56 % - 56 mL 4 H2O - - 44 mL

steps.title: Centrifuge at ≥ 6000 x g...
steps.instruction: Centrifuge at ≥ 6000 x g for 5 minutes and discard the flow-through. If the centrifuge cannot reach 6000 x g , a longer centrifugation (e.g., 5 minutes) will work.

steps.title: Add 500 μL Wash Buffer 2...
steps.instruction: Add 500 μL Wash Buffer 2 to each spin column and cover with a breathable seal.

steps.title: Carefully move the spin column plate to a new 0
steps.instruction: Carefully move the spin column plate to a new 0.5 mL DNA collection plate. The liquid level following the final wash will be close to the base of the spin column, so take care not to let it touch to prevent ethanol transfer to the soon-to-be eluted DNA.

steps.title: Wash Buffer 2 should be pH...
steps.instruction: Wash Buffer 2 should be pH ~7 and comprised of the following reagents: A B C D 1 Reagent Required concentration in buffer Chemical molarity Amount per 100 mL 2 Ethanol (100 %) 70 % 58.44 70 mL 3 1 M Tris-Hcl 10 mM - 1 mL 4 H2O - - 29 mL

steps.title: Centrifuge at ≥ 6000 x g...
steps.instruction: Centrifuge at ≥ 6000 x g for 15 minutes and discard the flow-through. If the centrifuge cannot reach 20,000 x g , a longer centrifugation (e.g., 15 minutes) will work.

steps.title: Add 200 μL Elution Buffer directly...
steps.instruction: Add 200 μL Elution Buffer directly to the silica membrane and leave it at room temperature for 5 minutes, covering with a breathable seal.

steps.title: Elution Buffer should be pH ~7...
steps.instruction: Elution Buffer should be pH ~7 and comprised of the following reagents: A B C D 1 Reagent Required concentration in buffer Chemical molarity Amount per 100 mL 2 1 M Tris-Hcl 10 mM - 1 mL 3 H2O - - 99 mL

steps.title: Centrifuge at ≥ 6000 x g for 2 minutes
steps.instruction: Centrifuge at ≥ 6000 x g for 2 minutes. The DNA is now in the collection plate and can be taken forward to amplification. Steps 20-21 can be repeated for increased DNA yield but a lower overall concentration. If the centrifuge cannot reach 6000 x g , a longer centrifugation (e.g., 5 minutes) will work, although should not be necessary for this step.

steps.title: Grind the larvae in a Geno/Grinder...
steps.instruction: Grind the larvae in a Geno/Grinder 2010 at full speed (1750 RPM) for 4 minutes. If the larvae are not fully homogenised, repeat this step.

steps.title: Centrifuge at 4,000 g for 2 minutes
steps.instruction: Centrifuge at 4,000 g for 2 minutes.

steps.title: Remove the ball bearings using a...
steps.instruction: Remove the ball bearings using a magnet, taking care not to cross-contaminate the samples.

steps.title: To the lysate, add 1 mL...
steps.instruction: To the lysate, add 1 mL of freshly-prepared Proteinase Buffer master mix of Lysis Buffer 2 and proteinase K (detailed in the sub-steps below) and vortex to mix.

steps.title: Lysis Buffer 2 should be pH...
steps.instruction: Lysis Buffer 2 should be pH 9 and comprised of the following reagents: A B C D 1 Reagent Required concentration in buffer Chemical molarity Amount per 100 mL 2 Sodium chloride 120 mM 58.44 0.701 g 3 1 M Tris-Hcl 50 mM - 5 mL 4 0.5 M EDTA 20 mM - 4 mL 5 SDS 3 % - 3 g 6 H2O - - 91 mL

steps.title: Per sample, the Proteinase Buffer master mix should comprise
steps.instruction: Per sample, the Proteinase Buffer master mix should comprise: A B 1 Reagent Amount per plate 2 Lysis solution 2 102.4 mL 3 Proteinase K (10 mg/mL) 3.168 mL

steps.title: Incubate at 37 °C overnight (12-16...
steps.instruction: Incubate at 37 °C overnight (12-16 hours) All proteins including Thaumetopoeitin are now inactivated.

steps.title: Centrifuge at 4000 x g for 4 minutes
steps.instruction: Centrifuge at 4000 x g for 4 minutes.

steps.title: Transfer 1 mL of the supernatant to a clean 2
steps.instruction: Transfer 1 mL of the supernatant to a clean 2.2 mL deep-well 96-well plate for archiving/backup and retain the rest in the original plate.

steps.title: Centrifuge at 2000 x g for 4 minutes
steps.instruction: Centrifuge at 2000 x g for 4 minutes.

steps.title: Transfer 200 μL of the supernatant to a clean 2
steps.instruction: Transfer 200 μL of the supernatant to a clean 2.2 mL deep-well 96-well plate. The remaining lysate can now be stored at -20 °C for backup/future work.

steps.title: Add one 3 mm hardened carbon...
steps.instruction: Add one 3 mm hardened carbon steel bead to each well. Beads are usually shipped coated in manufacturing oil (especially the carbon steel beads). To remove this, place beads in a borosilicate glass beaker or Duran bottle with plastic pouring lip and lid removed then bake for at least 12 hours at 250 °C.

steps.title: Using a sterile scalpel and forceps...
steps.instruction: Using a sterile scalpel and forceps , prepare each sample for molecular analysis by scraping the panel twice across a distance of 5 cm at each of the five pre-designated locations . Place all the scraped biofouling material from the panel into a microcentrifuge tube and label it with the panel information. Between each sample, thoroughly decontaminate dissection tools and any surfaces used (e.g., Petri dish) with Chemgene (or a similar DNA-degrading disinfectant), rinsing with molecular biology-grade water and 100 % ethanol, and wiping dry with sterile tissue.

steps.title: Transfer individual samples into 96-well plates (ideally deep-well, e
steps.instruction: Transfer individual samples into 96-well plates (ideally deep-well, e.g., 2.2 mL, with fixable lids for grinding/washing). Consider the distribution of experimental controls ahead of subsequent steps to streamline downstream liquid handling. If pausing before proceeding to homogenisation and lysis, seal the 96-well plate and store it at -20 °C until ready. When subsequently defrosting it, check that the TNES does not precipitate. If it does, gently warm the plate to 50-60 °C .

steps.title: Centrifuge the plate at 2000 x...
steps.instruction: Centrifuge the plate at 2000 x g, Room temperature, 2 minutes .

steps.title: Add 100 µL TNES lysis buffer...
steps.instruction: Add 100 µL TNES lysis buffer to each well of a 96-well plate. For the TNES buffer, follow the recipe provided by BOMB-Bio ( 100 millimolar (mM) Tris-HCl, 52 millimolar (mM) NaCl, 10 millimolar (mM) EDTA, 10 Mass / % volume SDS).

steps.title: Grind the samples in a tissue...
steps.instruction: Grind the samples in a tissue grinder/homogeniser/lyser at 1750 rpm, Room temperature, 1 minute .

steps.title: Add 10 µL 20 mg/mL papain to each well
steps.instruction: Add 10 µL 20 mg/mL papain to each well.

steps.title: Incubate overnight (~ 16:00:00 ) at...
steps.instruction: Incubate overnight (~ 16:00:00 ) at 37 °C or 02:00:00 at 56 °C .

steps.title: The DNA extraction protocol is largely...
steps.instruction: The DNA extraction protocol is largely adapted from the BOMB-Bio tissue nucleic acid extraction protocol . See their documentation for additional details. Oberacker P, Stepper P, Bond DM, Höhn S, Focken J, Meyer V, Schelle L, Sugrue VJ, Jeunen GJ, Moser T, Hore SR, von Meyenn F, Hipp K, Hore TA, Jurkowski TP.

steps.title: To a new plate, or to...
steps.instruction: To a new plate, or to individual tubes (depending on the compatibility of plasticware and magnetic tube/plate racks), add 60 µL of the lysate from STEP 10, 120 µL of 1.5X GITC buffer , 120 µL of 1 mg/mL SeraMag Speed Beads in TE ( 10 millimolar (mM) Tris and 1 millimolar (mM) EDTA) and 240 µL isopropanol . Mix by pipetting or vortexing 1500 rpm, Room temperature, 1 minute . For the 1.5X GITC buffer, follow the recipe provided by BOMB-Bio ( 6 Molarity (M) GITC, 75 millimolar (mM) Tris-HCl, 3 % volume sarkosyl, 30 millimolar (mM) EDTA, 0.15 % volume antifoam). When working with magnetic bead solutions, ensure they are at room temperature and fully mixed, with no residue at the bottom of the container.

steps.title: Incubate at Room temperature for 00:05:00
steps.instruction: Incubate at Room temperature for 00:05:00 .

steps.title: Place on a magnetic stand for 00:05:00
steps.instruction: Place on a magnetic stand for 00:05:00 .

steps.title: Remove all but 5 µL of...
steps.instruction: Remove all but 5 µL of the mixture from each well via pipette without disturbing the beads, which should be settled on the magnet. We recommend leaving 5 µL behind simply to avoid pipetting the beads themselves, but this can be avoided with care and experience.

steps.title: Add 400 µL 80 % volume ethanol to each well
steps.instruction: Add 400 µL 80 % volume ethanol to each well.

steps.title: Remove the ethanol entirely and discard it
steps.instruction: Remove the ethanol entirely and discard it.

steps.title: Remove the ethanol as completely as...
steps.instruction: Remove the ethanol as completely as possible by pipetting and discard it . Allow the beads to air-dry until the aggregation of magnetic beads transitions from 'glossy' (shiny reflection of light) to 'matte' (dull dark brown mass), but not so long that it dries completely (i.e., begins to turn a rusty red and shows cracks).

steps.title: Add 105 µL molecular grade water...
steps.instruction: Add 105 µL molecular grade water to each well, shake at 1500 rpm, Room temperature, 1 minute and incubate at Room temperature for 00:05:00 .

steps.title: Place on a magnetic stand for 00:05:00
steps.instruction: Place on a magnetic stand for 00:05:00 .

steps.title: Remove 100 µL of the supernatant...
steps.instruction: Remove 100 µL of the supernatant and place it into a new tube. This will be the sample used for PCR.

steps.title: Add 400 µL isopropanol to each well
steps.instruction: Add 400 µL isopropanol to each well.

steps.title: Remove the isopropanol entirely and discard it
steps.instruction: Remove the isopropanol entirely and discard it.

steps.title: Add a further 400 µL 80...
steps.instruction: Add a further 400 µL 80 % volume ethanol to each well.

steps.title: Store the eluted DNA at -20...
steps.instruction: Store the eluted DNA at -20 °C until ready for subsequent steps.

steps.title: Decide how samples will be distributed...
steps.instruction: Decide how samples will be distributed across plates (but don't distribute the DNA yet). Consider including a negative control in each row and column to detect any contaminants in each forward and reverse primer if using tagged primers (only necessary if sequencing downstream). Among these wells, include any DNA extraction negative controls . Include positive controls (extracted DNA from species known to amplify with the primers), perhaps one adjacent to negative controls and the other adjacent only to samples (but both on separate rows and columns). If sequencing downstream, include blank controls (ideally wells into which no reagents or at least no primers are added), perhaps one adjacent to negative controls and the other adjacent only to samples (but both on separate rows and columns). If using multiple PCR primer pairs, familiarise yourself with the annealing temperatures for each and prepare separate PCR plates for each. For optimal accuracy, consider running replicates of each reaction (e.g., triplicates). Our recommended PCR plate layout, which could be adopted here for streamlining downstream. Created in BioRender. Cuff, J. (2025) https://BioRender.com/yjynd1m

steps.title: Prepare enough PCR mastermix for each sample
steps.instruction: Prepare enough PCR mastermix for each sample. For a full plate, the below values will usually suffice (with some overage to account for pipetting error), but check your specific Taq polymerase mix for any differences: %s %s These values are for 20 µL reaction volumes, which have been demonstrated to be effective for diagnostic PCR and community metabarcoding. Consider running them in triplicate for more accurate results.

steps.title: For 20 µL reaction volumes, this...
steps.instruction: For 20 µL reaction volumes, this will subsequently involve adding 10 µL MyFi high-fidelity hot-start Taq polymerase and 8.6 µL molecular grade water to each well, followed by forward and reverse primers at a final reaction concentration of 0.2 micromolar (µM) (e.g., 0.4 µL each of 10 micromolar (µM) primer stock) to each corresponding well. It is possible to do this with a multichannel pipette for ease and speed.

steps.title: Add 1 µL DNA diluted to...
steps.instruction: Add 1 µL DNA diluted to 1:20 in nuclease free water to each corresponding sample or positive control well, and 20 µL nuclease free water to each negative control other than extraction negative control(s).

steps.title: Distribute one drop of mineral oil...
steps.instruction: Distribute one drop of mineral oil into each well of the PCR plate(s) (~ 20 µL ). This can be achieved by taking a large volume of mineral oil into the pipette tip and then gently depressing the plunger so that a drop forms and falls from the tip into each well.Mineral oil improves sealing of reactions by preventing evaporation and condensation. By reducing evaporation and thus loss of product, this also reduces potential cross-contamination.

steps.title: Briefly centrifuge the plate to ensure...
steps.instruction: Briefly centrifuge the plate to ensure that the oil is above the PCR mix and everything is at the bottom of each well without air bubbles.

steps.title: Load the PCR plate into a thermocycler
steps.instruction: Load the PCR plate into a thermocycler . Ensure that the temperature regime matches the enzyme used (including any heat activation for hot-start Taq) and that the annealing temperature matches the PCR primers used. Given differences between labs and samples, and inaccuracies in temperature calibration, considering running a temperature gradient PCR with known samples to check the specificity of your PCR primers.

steps.title: Run your PCR programme
steps.instruction: Run your PCR programme .

steps.title: The samples should now be checked...
steps.instruction: The samples should now be checked for successful amplification, contamination in negatives and any secondary banding. Gel electrophoresis will achieve this, but digital systems like the Qiagen Qiaxcel will do this faster and generate amplicon-specific DNA concentration data (which is useful for equimolar pooling if sequencing).

steps.title: If the sample detection is positive...
steps.instruction: If the sample detection is positive , the target species of the specific primer used is probably there, as its DNA has been detected in the sample. If a positive detection occurs, further methods of identification (e.g. visual methods using settlement panels or rapid assessment surveys) should be employed to validate results.

steps.title: Replication strategy Deploy five units per site to allow replication
steps.instruction: Replication strategy Deploy five units per site to allow replication. Within each site, choose tie-on points that minimise the risk of disturbance and avoid operational interference.

steps.title: Prepare a gel tank containing sufficient...
steps.instruction: Prepare a gel tank containing sufficient borax buffer to submerge the gel .

steps.title: Prepare a 2 Mass / %...
steps.instruction: Prepare a 2 Mass / % volume agarose gel for the samples to go into (detailed in the below substeps).

steps.title: Add 2 µL of each sample...
steps.instruction: Add 2 µL of each sample to a new plate and add 2 µL of 1X DNA dye for visualisation of the products in the gel.

steps.title: Prepare a gel mould with sufficient...
steps.instruction: Prepare a gel mould with sufficient comb tips for the number of samples to be run. Ensure these are spaced out enough to allow the gel to run and separate without running into another well.

steps.title: Determine a suitable size (and therefore...
steps.instruction: Determine a suitable size (and therefore volume) of gel based on the size of the tank available. For a 150 mL gel, weigh 3 g of agarose and add it to 150 mL of 1X borax buffer .

steps.title: Heat this mixture in a microwave...
steps.instruction: Heat this mixture in a microwave in a conical flask until it begins bubbling .

steps.title: Mix the solution by gently swirling...
steps.instruction: Mix the solution by gently swirling it and heat again until bubbling. Repeat this as necessary until there are no large lumps of agarose remaining . It can be beneficial to let the mixture cool for 00:00:30 before proceeding.

steps.title: Add 3 µL stain (e
steps.instruction: Add 3 µL stain (e.g., Gel Red) and mix by swirling.

steps.title: Pour the mixture into the gel...
steps.instruction: Pour the mixture into the gel mould and allow to set for 00:25:00 .

steps.title: Once set, place the gel into...
steps.instruction: Once set, place the gel into the tank and add 4 µL of DNA gel ladder to each side of the gel in each row, and then 4 µL of dyed PCR product to each other well. Ensure that the DNA will run in the correct direction when placing the gel in the tank. TIP: it will 'run to the red'.

steps.title: Once filled, run the gel power...
steps.instruction: Once filled, run the gel power pack at 300 V and 120 A for 00:20:00

steps.title: Visualise the gel under a UV...
steps.instruction: Visualise the gel under a UV or blue light in a gel dock to confirm amplification and a lack of contamination in negative controls. Ensure you are not exposed to UV by only using the UV light in a contained dock and/or with appropriate protective equipment.

steps.title: Pre-designate five locations to be scraped...
steps.instruction: Pre-designate five locations to be scraped and biofouling removed on each panel. Select points in the centre, left of centre, right of centre, above centre, and below centre. This will prevent bias towards heavily fouled areas of panels.

steps.title: Purpose
steps.instruction: Purpose: Construct and deploy a 5-panel UPVC settlement panel unit to hang at 1 m depth.

steps.title: Construction Material preparation and unit assembly
steps.instruction: Construction Material preparation and unit assembly. Best to be conducted in a workspace prior to site visits.

steps.title: Prepare materials and workspace
steps.instruction: Prepare materials and workspace . Wear PPE. Lay out UPVC panels and tools.

steps.title: Cut panels
steps.instruction: Cut panels . Ensure each panel is 14 × 14 × 1cm . If using supplier-cut panels, verify dimensions.

steps.title: Roughen surfaces
steps.instruction: Roughen surfaces . Lightly abrade one face of each panel with sandpaper to increase the surface area for settlement 00:00:30 .

steps.title: Deburr edges
steps.instruction: Deburr edges . Using sandpaper, remove sharp edges present after cutting and sanding.

steps.title: Drill holes
steps.instruction: Drill holes . Drill a 5 mm hole 5 mm in from each corner of every panel.

steps.title: Arrange panels
steps.instruction: Arrange panels . Place the five panels with sanded surfaces facing out into a pentagonal formation so that adjoining edges meet.

steps.title: Join panels
steps.instruction: Join panels . At each panel-to-panel corner, pass cable ties through the aligned corner holes and tighten. Use two ties per panel (total 10 cable ties ) for secure joining. Trim excess tails.

steps.title: Create top attachment loops
steps.instruction: Create top attachment loops . Choose three corner joins on the top side of the pentagon (spaced roughly evenly). Thread one cable tie through each selected corner (use 3 additional zip ties ). Leave the cable ties unattached for connection to the mooring line.

steps.title: Prepare mooring line length
steps.instruction: Prepare mooring line length . Measure the vertical distance from the planned tie-on point to the water surface (call this D). Calculate total rope length as D + 3.0 m (allows room for knotting and the weight). On the rope, from the tie-on end, mark: the water surface position at D from the tie-on end; the unit position at D + 1.0 m (this is where the unit should hang, i.e., 1m depth); weight position at D + 1.5 m (where the weight will be attached, excess line to secure weight).

steps.title: Tie a loop at the unit position
steps.instruction: Tie a loop at the unit position . At the mark for D + 1.0 m , tie a bowline (or equivalent secure loop) in the rope.

steps.title: Attach the unit to the rope
steps.instruction: Attach the unit to the rope . Pass the three zip ties from the unit through the bowline loop and secure (close the cable ties so the unit is held by the rope loop). Ensure the unit hangs level.

steps.title: Attach weight
steps.instruction: Attach weight . At the free (lower) end of the rope, securely attach the weight (house brick in a bag or other secure method) by splicing or firm knotting.

steps.title: Final checks
steps.instruction: Final checks . Lower the unit (or simulate) to confirm the top of the unit sits 1.0 m below the water surface . Confirm all cable ties are tight and trimmed, knots are secure, and the assembly hangs freely.

steps.title: Attach the mooring line of the...
steps.instruction: Attach the mooring line of the unit securely to the tie-on point using a strong knot (e.g., bowline). If unsure, reinforce with additional knots or zip ties.

steps.title: Identify a secure tie-on point such...
steps.instruction: Identify a secure tie-on point such as a cleat or structural hole beneath a pontoon. Always maintain three points of contact with the structure. If working under pontoons, lying flat may reduce the risk of slips or falls.

steps.title: Once the mooring line is secured,...
steps.instruction: Once the mooring line is secured, slowly lower the unit into the water , weight first, ensuring it hangs freely.

steps.title: Site selection
steps.instruction: Site selection

steps.title: Select suitable sites where substrate for...
steps.instruction: Select suitable sites where substrate for biofouling growth and tie-on locations are available(e.g., marinas, bridges, pontoons). Deployment of units from pontoons is preferable to ensure a constant water depth is maintained at all times.

steps.title: Safety preparations
steps.instruction: Safety preparations

steps.title: Avoid areas with excessive hydrodynamic activity...
steps.instruction: Avoid areas with excessive hydrodynamic activity or wave action, which may damage or dislodge units.

steps.title: Consult stakeholders (e
steps.instruction: Consult stakeholders (e.g., harbourmasters, site managers) to ensure deployment doesnot interfere with site operations and to maintain/build relationships.

steps.title: Wear suitable PPE , including gloves,...
steps.instruction: Wear suitable PPE , including gloves, sturdy footwear, and lifejackets if workingnear water.

steps.title: Follow institutional fieldwork safety guidelines (e
steps.instruction: Follow institutional fieldwork safety guidelines (e.g., buddy system, riskassessments).

steps.title: Deployment Method
steps.instruction: Deployment Method

steps.title: Confirm the unit is suspended at...
steps.instruction: Confirm the unit is suspended at 1 m depth (adjust rope length if necessary).

steps.title: Final Checks
steps.instruction: Final Checks

steps.title: Label and record
steps.instruction: Label and record . Mark the unit with a unique ID, and record the deployment date, GPS/position, and any observations for future retrieval or maintenance in the field notebook.

steps.title: Check all knots are secure and the weight is fixed
steps.instruction: Check all knots are secure and the weight is fixed.

steps.title: Purpose
steps.instruction: Purpose: Provide clear and consistent methods for monitoring settlement panel units and retrieving samples for visual and eDNA analysis.

steps.title: Mark/identify one panel per unit for...
steps.instruction: Mark/identify one panel per unit for use as a "one-month panel" (discussed further in the monitoring and sample retrieval section).

steps.title: Maintain consistent timing of monitoring visits (e
steps.instruction: Maintain consistent timing of monitoring visits (e.g., monthly intervals) to ensure comparability across the study period.

steps.title: Purpose
steps.instruction: Purpose: Deploy constructed settlement panel units safely and consistently at selected field sites.

steps.title: Follow the safety preparations discussed in the panel deployment
steps.instruction: Follow the safety preparations discussed in the panel deployment.

steps.title: Unit retrieval for monitoring
steps.instruction: Unit retrieval for monitoring

steps.title: At each monitoring visit, carefully lift...
steps.instruction: At each monitoring visit, carefully lift each fouling panel unit from the water. Ensure lifting procedures are followed and three contact points are maintained when bending to retrieve units.

steps.title: Place the unit on a flat...
steps.instruction: Place the unit on a flat surface with good natural light.

steps.title: Photography
steps.instruction: Photography

steps.title: Photograph all five panels of each unit
steps.instruction: Photograph all five panels of each unit.

steps.title: Take images in natural light at two distances
steps.instruction: Take images in natural light at two distances: 15 cm and 30 cm from the panel surface. Include a scale card or ruler in each image.

steps.title: Sample/Panel retrieval
steps.instruction: Sample/Panel retrieval

steps.title: Ensure images are sharp, evenly lit,...
steps.instruction: Ensure images are sharp, evenly lit, and labelled in accordance with unit ID, date, and location.

steps.title: Each unit contains five panels
steps.instruction: Each unit contains five panels : four continuous panels and one one-month panel . Continuous panels: Starting from Monitoring Visit 2 , remove one continuous panel per visit. Do not remove any continuous panels during Monitoring Visit 1 . One-month panels: At each monitoring visit ( starting from Visit 1 ), remove the designated one-monthpanel. Replace it immediately with a new, clean panel.

steps.title: Panel removal and replacement Cut the...
steps.instruction: Panel removal and replacement Cut the zip ties securing the panel. Place the panel into the designated and labelled clear plastic bag . (labelled with site/location, unit ID, panel type (continuous or one-month), and date) Place labelled bags into an icebox with ice packs. Attach a replacement panel with zip ties to the unit if a one-month panel has been removed. If a continuous panel has been removed at its designated time point, secure the two adjacent panels together to reform the unit. Following the second monitoring visit, the number of panels on the unit will decrease by one at each monitoring visit (e.g., monitoring visit 1 = pentagon, monitoring visit 2 = square, monitoring visit 3 = triangle, monitoring visit 4 = panels back to back, monitoring visit 5 = removal of the unit).

steps.title: Storage - Store all removed panels...
steps.instruction: Storage - Store all removed panels at –20 °C until required for eDNA analysis.

steps.title: Purpose
steps.instruction: Purpose: To prepare biofouling samples collected on the settlement units for DNA extraction.

steps.title: Purpose
steps.instruction: Purpose: Extract DNA from the samples prepared in the previous section (steps 16-25) to be tested against species-specific primers.

steps.title: Purpose
steps.instruction: Purpose: Test extracted DNA against species-specific primers for target invasive species.

steps.title: Purpose
steps.instruction: Purpose: Visually identify positive PCR detections of invasive species following species-specific primer use.

description: Notes & tips - Prepare extra rope, panels and spare units for field repairs. Notes & Tips Deployment from pontoons is preferred, as they rise and fall with tides, so units consistently remain at 1 m depth. Carry spare rope, cable ties, and a spare unit in case of field repairs or losses. Always maintain clear communication with site stakeholders and follow local safety regulations. Notes & tips Handle panels carefully to avoid scraping or damaging biofouling. Handle panels from the back (the non-target side) and clean hands before and between panels to prevent cross-contamination. Check photographs in the field before progressing to ensure clarity and completeness of each image. See forthcoming publications for further details on image analysis and detailed methodology.
